subject: EU Law
lesson_title: Rights Conferred: Equal Treatment, Social Advantages, and Family Members

questions.question: The principle of non-discrimination on grounds of nationality in employment is the cornerstone of Article 45 TFEU. Which of the following forms of discrimination are covered?

options.A: Only direct discrimination — expressly applying different rules to foreign workers
options.B: Both direct discrimination (expressly applying less favourable rules to foreign workers) and indirect discrimination (applying facially neutral rules that disproportionately disadvantage foreign workers without objective justification)
options.C: Only discrimination in pay — other employment conditions are governed by national law
options.D: Only discrimination by public employers — private sector employment is outside Article 45

questions.explanation: Article 45 TFEU and Regulation 492/2011 prohibit both direct and indirect discrimination. Direct discrimination is explicit — different rules applied because the worker is from another member state. Indirect discrimination occurs where a facially neutral rule disproportionately disadvantages migrant EU workers — for example, requirements of prior residence in the host state as a condition for benefits (Sotgiu v Deutsche Bundespost [1974]), or language requirements beyond what the job genuinely requires. Indirect discrimination can be justified by objective reasons unconnected with nationality and proportionate — but 'objective justification' is strictly scrutinised. Both forms of discrimination are prohibited by Article 7(1) of Regulation 492/2011.

questions.question: Article 7(2) of Regulation 492/2011 guarantees migrant EU workers the same 'social and tax advantages' as nationals. In Even [1979] (C-207/78), the CJEU defined 'social advantages' as:

options.A: Only benefits provided under national social security legislation
options.B: Advantages which, whether or not linked to a contract of employment, are generally granted to national workers primarily because of their objective status as workers or by virtue of the mere fact of their residence on the national territory
options.C: Only advantages provided to workers by their employers as employment benefits
options.D: Only state benefits linked to contributions made to the national insurance system

questions.explanation: Even defined 'social advantages' very broadly — encompassing any advantage which is granted to national workers primarily because of their status as workers or by virtue of residence in the national territory, whether or not linked to the employment contract. The CJEU has applied this definition to include: reduced-price rail cards for large families (Even itself — Belgian reduced rail fares for workers in foreign armed forces); the right to use one's own language in judicial proceedings (Mutsch); minimum income guarantees; child-rearing allowances; and scholarships for children's higher education. The definition deliberately extends beyond narrowly 'employment-related' benefits to include the full range of advantages that nationals take for granted.

questions.question: The right to equality in access to housing under Regulation 492/2011 Article 9 means:

options.A: Migrant EU workers are entitled to priority access to social housing over nationals
options.B: Migrant EU workers have the right to the same access to housing as national workers of the host state — including the right to own housing and to enjoy any benefits and rights that national workers enjoy in connection with housing
options.C: Migrant EU workers may only access private rented housing, not social housing
options.D: Housing rights under Regulation 492/2011 apply only after five years of lawful residence

questions.explanation: Article 9 of Regulation 492/2011 provides that a migrant EU worker has the same rights and benefits as national workers as regards housing, including ownership of the housing needed for the worker. They may enlist on housing lists in the region where they are employed, on the same conditions as nationals, and their family members are treated as national families for the purpose of housing benefits. This provision aims to ensure migrant workers are not disadvantaged in competing for accommodation — practical integration in the host state depends heavily on adequate housing access.

questions.question: Family members of EU workers derive residence rights under Directive 2004/38. Who qualifies as a 'family member' entitled to join the EU worker in the host member state?

options.A: Only the worker's spouse and biological children under 18
options.B: The spouse or registered partner; direct descendants under 21 or dependent; dependent direct relatives in the ascending line (parents, grandparents) of both the worker and spouse; additionally, Article 3(2) requires member states to 'facilitate' entry of more distant relatives and partners in a durable relationship
options.C: Any family member who has lived with the worker for more than 12 months prior to migration
options.D: Only family members who are themselves EU citizens

questions.explanation: Directive 2004/38 Article 2(2) defines the core family members entitled to accompany or join an EU worker: (a) spouse; (b) registered partner (where the host state recognises registered partnerships); (c) direct descendants of either the worker or their spouse who are under 21 or dependent; (d) dependent direct relatives in the ascending line of either the worker or their spouse. Article 3(2) additionally requires member states to 'facilitate' entry and residence of other family members (wider family or dependants) and partners in durable relationships, though Article 3(2) does not confer the automatic rights of Article 2(2) family members — member states must give their situation 'extensive examination'.

questions.question: Can a third-country national (non-EU citizen) who is a family member of an EU worker claim residence rights in the EU worker's host member state?

options.A: No — only EU citizens can claim residence rights under EU free movement law
options.B: Yes — Directive 2004/38 grants third-country national family members of EU workers derivative residence rights in the host member state; their rights derive from their family relationship with the EU worker, not from their own nationality
options.C: Yes, but only if they have already been legally resident in the EU for five years
options.D: Yes, but only for spouses — other family members must be EU citizens to claim rights

questions.explanation: Third-country national (TCN) family members of EU workers have derivative EU free movement rights. Their rights are parasitic on the EU worker's rights — they derive from the family relationship with the exercising EU citizen. This was established pre-Directive through MRAX [2002] and Metock [2008]. In Metock, the CJEU confirmed that a TCN spouse of an EU worker can claim residence rights in the host state regardless of whether they were previously lawfully resident in another EU member state — overturning the more restrictive approach the Court had taken in Akrich [2003]. The Directive requires family reunion as a practical necessity for the exercise of free movement rights.

questions.question: In Christini v SNCF [1975] (C-32/75), the French state railway refused to grant a reduced fare card for large families to an Italian national widow of a deceased French railway worker. The CJEU held:

options.A: That the benefit was not a 'social advantage' because the worker had died
options.B: That the reduced rail fare card was a social advantage under Article 7(2) of Regulation 1612/68 and the surviving spouse (as successor of the worker) was entitled to it on the same terms as French nationals — the right extended beyond the worker themselves
options.C: That social advantages under Regulation 1612/68 can only be claimed while the worker is alive
options.D: That the widow had to apply for French citizenship to access the benefit after her husband's death

questions.explanation: Christini is significant for establishing that social advantages under Article 7(2) are not limited to the worker personally but can extend to family members as the practical beneficiaries of the advantage. The SNCF family railcard was a social advantage linked to the worker's status — the Court held it could not be denied to the worker's family simply because the worker had died. The underlying rationale is that the free movement framework aims to facilitate genuine integration of workers and their families in the host state — denying family-related benefits would undermine this objective. The extension to family members is also confirmed in Article 7 read with Article 2(2) of Directive 2004/38.

questions.question: Regulation 492/2011 Article 7(3) guarantees that migrant EU workers have access to vocational training 'under the same conditions as national workers'. The CJEU in Lair [1988] held this includes:

options.A: Only job-specific on-the-job training provided by employers
options.B: University-level education and training grants where there is a link between the education and the worker's previous employment — a migrant worker retaining worker status during vocational training has the right to equal access to maintenance grants on the same terms as nationals
options.C: Only professional qualifications recognition — not educational course access
options.D: Vocational training provided only within the worker's current employment

questions.explanation: Lair extended Article 7(3) to cover university education where there is continuity between the previous employment and the course undertaken (vocational training in a broad sense). A migrant worker who retains worker status while undergoing vocational training has the right to receive maintenance grants and other educational benefits on the same terms as national workers — not just access to the course. This was a significant expansion: it meant that EU free movement law required equal treatment in student funding as well as course access. The subsequent development of EU citizenship has further expanded educational rights for EU citizens beyond workers, but Lair remains the foundation for worker-based educational rights.

questions.question: The right to work of a non-EU spouse of an EU worker (exercising free movement rights) was confirmed in which case?

options.B: Gül v Regierungspräsident Düsseldorf [1986] (C-131/85) — a non-EU doctor married to an EU worker was held to have the right to take up employment in the host state under the same conditions as the EU worker's national counterpart
options.C: Carpenter v Secretary of State for the Home Department [2002]

questions.explanation: Gül established that the non-EU spouse of an EU worker exercising free movement rights has the right to take up employment in the host member state. This derived from the family reunification provisions of Regulation 1612/68 (now Regulation 492/2011 and Directive 2004/38). The rationale: free movement rights would be a hollow guarantee if workers could not bring their families and families could not work — the practical ability to establish family life in the host state requires that family members can take up employment. Article 23 of Directive 2004/38 now expressly confirms that all family members (including non-EU nationals) have the right to take up employment in the host member state.

questions.question: Directive 2004/38 Article 16 grants a right of permanent residence to EU citizens and their family members after five years of lawful continuous residence in the host member state. Which of the following is NOT a condition for acquiring permanent residence under Article 16?

options.A: Five years of lawful and continuous residence
options.B: Being economically active throughout the five-year period — persons who are not workers, self-employed, or students cannot acquire permanent residence
options.C: The absence of an expulsion decision against the person
options.D: Not being absent from the host member state for more than two consecutive years

questions.explanation: Article 16 of Directive 2004/38 grants permanent residence after five years of lawful continuous residence to ALL EU citizens and their family members — not only to economically active persons. Economic inactivity is not a bar to permanent residence after five years: economically inactive persons (including students, retired persons, and those on sufficient resources) who have resided lawfully for five years acquire permanent residence. The key requirements are: five years of continuous lawful residence; continuity is not broken by absences up to six months per year (or longer for specific reasons: military service, pregnancy, serious illness, study/vocational training, posting abroad); and not being subject to an expulsion decision.

questions.question: Article 14 of Directive 2004/38 provides that EU workers (and their families) retain their right of residence as long as they continue to be workers or self-employed. What is the rule for retaining worker status when temporarily unable to work?

options.A: Temporary inability to work always terminates worker status and the associated right of residence
options.B: Article 7(3) of Directive 2004/38 preserves worker status (and thus the right of residence) where the worker: is temporarily unable to work due to illness or accident; is in duly recorded involuntary unemployment after more than one year of employment (or after less than one year if employment terminated involuntarily); or has embarked on vocational training
options.C: Workers retain status only for three months of unemployment
options.D: Worker status is retained only where the worker registers with a public employment office within 30 days of becoming unemployed

questions.explanation: Article 7(3) of Directive 2004/38 codifies and expands the CJEU's case law on retention of worker status. The four situations where worker status is retained: (1) temporary incapacity due to illness or accident; (2) duly recorded involuntary unemployment after employment lasting more than one year — worker status and residence right retained indefinitely; (3) duly recorded involuntary unemployment after employment of less than one year — worker status retained for at least six months; (4) voluntary commencement of vocational training — except where employment ended involuntarily, training must be related to previous employment. These provisions prevent the loss of residence rights due to circumstances beyond the worker's control.

questions.question: Carpenter v Secretary of State for the Home Department [2002] (C-60/00) concerned a British service provider (Mr Carpenter) whose Philippine wife (without leave to remain) cared for their children while he provided services across EU member states. The CJEU held:

options.A: That Mrs Carpenter had no EU law rights as she was a third-country national not married to an EU worker
options.B: That Mr Carpenter's right to provide services under Article 56 TFEU was engaged, and expelling his wife would hinder that right; respecting family life (Article 8 ECHR / now EU Charter Article 7) justified Mrs Carpenter's continued residence — the UK could not expel her without violating EU fundamental rights
options.C: That third-country national spouses of service providers have automatic permanent residence rights
options.D: That family unity cannot justify derogations from immigration control measures

questions.explanation: Carpenter is a key case at the intersection of free movement of services and fundamental rights. The CJEU held that Mr Carpenter exercised Article 56 rights (providing services to clients in other EU member states) and that his wife's care of the children enabled him to do so. Deporting Mrs Carpenter would interfere with Mr Carpenter's Article 56 rights by disrupting his family life in a way incompatible with the fundamental right to respect for family life (Article 8 ECHR). This case illustrates that EU free movement rights can be used to protect third-country national family members even where they do not independently qualify under Directive 2004/38 — particularly where fundamental rights are engaged.

questions.question: A migrant EU worker receives identical pay to national workers in the host state but is denied a housing allowance available only to persons who have resided in the member state for at least three years. This is:

options.A: Lawful — residence conditions are a legitimate national policy choice
options.B: Indirect discrimination contrary to Article 45 TFEU and Article 7(2) of Regulation 492/2011 — a prior residence requirement for a social advantage disproportionately disadvantages migrant workers who, by definition, have recently arrived in the member state
options.C: Lawful — provided the three-year residence requirement applies to nationals and foreign workers equally
options.D: Direct discrimination, since the rule explicitly targets non-residents who are by definition foreign workers

questions.explanation: Prior residence requirements for social advantages constitute indirect discrimination against migrant EU workers. Though facially neutral (applying equally to any non-resident regardless of nationality), the requirement disproportionately burdens migrant workers because they cannot satisfy it upon arrival in the host state — whereas nationals can typically satisfy it from birth. This was established in Sotgiu v Deutsche Bundespost [1974] — rules requiring 'place of origin' or long prior residence as a condition for benefits are indirectly discriminatory. Such requirements can only be justified if objectively necessary and proportionate — mere convenience of administration is insufficient. Residence conditions used to restrict social advantages to long-established residents are typically unjustifiable.

questions.question: The children of an EU worker (whether or not the worker is still employed) who are in education in the host member state may rely on Article 10 of Regulation 492/2011 even if the worker has left the host state. This right was confirmed in:

options.B: Baumbast and R [2002] (C-413/99) — the CJEU held that children of a former EU worker have an independent right to continue their education in the host state under Article 12 of Regulation 1612/68 (now Article 10 of Regulation 492/2011), and the primary carer retains a residence right to enable this education to continue

questions.explanation: Baumbast and R is a landmark case with two key holdings: (1) EU citizenship confers directly effective rights (Article 21 TFEU); and (2) the children of a former EU worker (Mr Baumbast had ceased being employed in the UK) retained the right to continue their education in the UK under Article 12 of Regulation 1612/68, now Article 10 of Regulation 492/2011. The Court further held that the primary carer — in R's case, the non-EU national divorced mother — retained a right of residence in the host state to enable the children to continue their education. This education right is independent of the parent's current worker status and independent of EU citizenship — it derives directly from the Regulation.
